elements.sent-2411-001: Recent advancements in Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampling and surrogate modelling have significantly enhanced the feasibility of Bayesian analysis across engineering fields.
elements.sent-2411-002: However, the selection and integration of surrogate models and cutting-edge MCMC algorithms, often depend on ad-hoc decisions.
elements.sent-2411-003: A systematic assessment of their combined influence on analytical accuracy and efficiency is notably lacking.
elements.sent-2411-004: The present work offers a comprehensive comparative study, employing a scalable case study in computational mechanics focused on the inference of spatially varying material parameters, that sheds light on the impact of methodological choices for surrogate modelling and sampling.
elements.sent-2411-005: We show that a priori training of the surrogate model introduces large errors in the posterior estimation even in low to moderate dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-006: We introduce a simple active learning strategy based on the path of the MCMC algorithm that is superior to all a priori trained models, and determine its training data requirements.
elements.sent-2411-007: We demonstrate that the choice of the MCMC algorithm has only a small influence on the amount of training data but no significant influence on the accuracy of the resulting surrogate model.
elements.sent-2411-008: Further, we show that the accuracy of the posterior estimation largely depends on the surrogate model, but not even a tailored surrogate guarantees convergence of the MCMC.
elements.sent-2411-009: Finally, we identify the forward model as the bottleneck in the inference process, not the MCMC algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-010: While related works focus on employing advanced MCMC algorithms, we demonstrate that the training data requirements render the surrogate modelling approach infeasible before the benefits of these gradient-based MCMC algorithms on cheap models can be reaped.
elements.sent-2411-011: In engineering mechanics, complex computational models, e.g. with the finite element method (FEM), are used to simulate material behaviour.
elements.sent-2411-012: The number of parameters for these models can be large, for instance if the material properties vary in space.
elements.sent-2411-013: These parameters often represent an internal state of the model that is difficult or impossible to measure directly.
elements.sent-2411-014: Therefore, an inverse problem must be solved to estimate these model parameters from indirect experimental measurements, commonly referred to as observations.
elements.sent-2411-015: In the nonelastic regime, these inverse problems are typically non-linear, non-convex, and ill-posed, but they can be regularised elegantly by adopting a Bayesian approach.
elements.sent-2411-016: This approach to inverse modelling employs Bayes' theorem to update the distribution of the parameters based on the observed data \cite{Dobrilla2023,Marsili2017 .}
elements.sent-2411-017: On the downside, Bayesian inverse modelling is computationally expensive, preventing its widespread application to large-scale problems.
elements.sent-2411-018: Recent advancements in Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampling and surrogate modelling have substantially improved the applicability of Bayesian analysis in these scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-019: Despite the potential of these innovations, the selection of surrogate models and integration with state-of-the-art MCMC algorithms often rely on ad-hoc choices.
elements.sent-2411-020: A systematic evaluation of their combined impact on overall performance remains scarce.
elements.sent-2411-021: This lack of clarity obscures the understanding of how each component impacts the overall effectiveness of the inference process.
elements.sent-2411-022: Addressing this gap, our study conducts a comparative analysis to elucidate the effects of the methodological decisions regarding surrogate modelling and sampling.
elements.sent-2411-023: In Bayesian inference, a prior distribution for the parameters is assumed and combined with a likelihood function.
elements.sent-2411-024: The likelihood measures the agreement of the model predictions with the observed system response.
elements.sent-2411-025: Following the Bayesian formalism, a posterior distribution for the parameters is obtained.
elements.sent-2411-026: For instance, when inferring material properties from measurements, the posterior distribution must be approximated due to the non-linear nature of the likelihood function, e.g. via sampling from the joint distribution of data and parameters.
elements.sent-2411-027: The workflow of a Bayesian analysis entails the following steps: 1) The sampling algorithm generates a new state, i.e. a realisation of the parameter vector.
elements.sent-2411-028: The bottleneck of the sampling effort is the evaluation of the forward model (step 2), which, in case of FEM-based inverse modelling, is an expensive simulator that maps the parameters to the observables.
elements.sent-2411-029: Steps 1 and 4 are implemented by the sampling algorithm and determine how often steps 2 and 3 are executed.
elements.sent-2411-030: The most commonly used sampling algorithm is the random walk Metropolis (RWM) algorithm due to its simplicity and guaranteed asymptotic convergence~\cite{Mengersen1996 .}
elements.sent-2411-031: However, its sampling efficiency decreases with the dimensionality of the parameter space~\cite{Roberts2001 .}
elements.sent-2411-032: One way to reduce the number of costly forward passes is by employing sampling algorithms that more efficiently produce uncorrelated samples from the posterior distribution.
elements.sent-2411-033: The first-order Metropolis adjusted Langevin algorithm~\cite{Roberts2002 , Hamiltonian Monte Carlo~\cite{Duane1987 , or their second-order manifold variants~\cite{Girolami2011 , as well as piecewise deterministic Markov process based algorithms~\cite{Bierkens2019 maintain high sampling efficiency in high-dimensional settings.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-034: Empirical studies of their performance on engineering problems~\cite{Girolami2011,Chong2017,Goodman2010 suggests their theoretical performance in terms of convergence rates on canonical distributions~\cite{Gelman1997,Roberts1998 translates well to practical settings.}}
elements.sent-2411-035: However, these algorithms require the gradient of the log-joint, or even higher derivatives thereof, which amounts to the computation of the model sensitivities with respect to its parameters.
elements.sent-2411-036: These quantities are rarely available for large-scale computational mechanics simulators, preventing a straightforward application of these algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-037: The gradients can be obtained with a finite difference scheme, but the computation of a finite difference stencil often negates the performance gains of the more advanced algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-038: Ensemble-based methods such as transitional (ensemble) Markov chain Monte Carlo (TMCMC, TEMCMC)~\cite{Ching2007, Lye2022 , or sequential Monte Carlo in the form of Bayesian subset simulation with structural liability methods \cite{Straub2015 show significant performance improvements over RWM in terms of sampling efficiency.}}
elements.sent-2411-039: While these algorithms do not require a differentiable forward model, they require at least $\mathcal{O (10^4)$ model evaluations to ensure convergent posterior statistics.}
elements.sent-2411-040: This would still render a Bayesian analysis infeasible, even when assuming only a few minutes of runtime per model evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-041: The other increasingly common strategy to reduce the computational cost of the sampling effort is the application of surrogate models.
elements.sent-2411-042: Surrogate models exploit the redundancy in the model predictions required for collecting posterior samples.
elements.sent-2411-043: Instead of running the expensive simulator in each MCMC step, a cheap-to-evaluate surrogate model is trained on a small number of solver calls.
elements.sent-2411-044: Learning a cheap mapping from the parameter domain to the observations (step 2) is one of these surrogate modelling approaches.
elements.sent-2411-045: In~\citet{Wu2020a , a neural network mapping from material parameters to strain paths takes the place of an iterative mean-field homogenisation algorithm for material property inference in the presence of plastic deformations.}
elements.sent-2411-046: All these approaches have in common that multiple models must be trained if the dataset consists of observations originating from fundamentally different conditions.
elements.sent-2411-047: The three distinct test scenarios in~\citet{Wu2020a motivate the training of three neural networks to reduce the complexity of individual tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-048: Further, the works employing polynomial chaos and Gaussian process models do not consider correlations between the outputs and train one model per scalar observation.
elements.sent-2411-049: While model training is typically not the computational bottleneck in the settings we consider here, tweaking the hyperparameters for the individual models can be cumbersome and time-consuming for the analyst.
elements.sent-2411-050: In step 3 of the workflow, the observations are funneled into the likelihood function to produce a scalar measure for the agreements of observations and model predictions for a given parameter vector.
elements.sent-2411-051: Therefore, it is appealing to use a surrogate for the likelihood function directly instead of for the observations, with the clear advantage that only one model must be trained.
elements.sent-2411-052: Following this idea~\citet{Chen2023 use a neural network to accelerate the parameter identification of an elastoplastic CuCrZr alloy.}
elements.sent-2411-053: Both~\citet{Drovandi2018 and~\citet{DelVal2022 employ a GP to efficiently infer stochastic rate constants in a gene network and material parameters governing the catalytic recombination, respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-054: However, targeting the likelihood function also introduces certain complexities.
elements.sent-2411-055: The likelihood incorporates all model non-linearity at once and might be more difficult of a target than the individual model predictions.
elements.sent-2411-056: Moreover, the likelihood has its own parameters, e.g. the observation noise, that one might be uncertain about.
elements.sent-2411-057: These parameters of the observation model can be inferred alongside the model parameters, but this increases the dimensionality of the input space for the surrogate model.
elements.sent-2411-058: Despite these challenges, we prioritise the likelihood approach in our study.
elements.sent-2411-059: This decision is driven by its broader applicability across various domains, as it allows for a more generalised framework for modelling in contrast to methods tailored to specific physical models.
elements.sent-2411-060: It should be noted that multi-fidelity MCMC~\cite{Zhang2018 and non-linear dimensionality reduction techniques~\cite{Dasgupta2024 have shown promising results for Bayesian inverse modelling.}}
elements.sent-2411-061: However, these methods come with their own set of modelling challenges and therefore remain beyond the scope of this work.
elements.sent-2411-062: For any choice of surrogate model, training data selection is crucial for the accuracy of the approximation.
elements.sent-2411-063: Most commonly, an a priori strategy is employed.
elements.sent-2411-064: The training points are either sampled from the prior, generated by Latin hypercube sampling~\cite{YuanHu2024, Thomas2022, Wu2020a, Chen2023 , or spread out on a grid~\cite{DelVal2022 .}}
elements.sent-2411-065: While the a priori approach might produce sufficiently accurate models in low stochastic dimensions, it is susceptible to the curse of dimensionality: the probability of being within a certain proximity of a training data point reduces exponentially with the dimensionality of the parameter space.
elements.sent-2411-066: The model accuracy is typically measured by the mean squared error (MSE) on a validation set.
elements.sent-2411-067: This set is generated with the same strategy as the training data.
elements.sent-2411-068: However, this MSE is not necessarily a good indicator of the quality of the surrogate model for a Bayesian analysis --- the surrogate model is only required to be accurate in regions of high posterior density.
elements.sent-2411-069: Active learning strategies, such as~\cite{Deveney2023, Kandasamy2015, Drovandi2018, Dinkel2024 , exploit this more localised requirement.}
elements.sent-2411-070: They probe the posterior distribution to collect more informative data points.
elements.sent-2411-071: It should be noted that surrogate models are generally not only cheap to evaluate, but also cheap to differentiate, enabling the application of gradient-based sampling algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-072: The aforementioned works which focus on surrogate models for Bayesian inference identify MCMC sampling as a bottleneck and employ a variety of algorithms: RWM in~\cite{Wu2020a, DelVal2022 ; HMC or its No U-Turn (NUTS) variant ~\cite{Deveney2023, YuanHu2024, Thomas2022 ; TMCMC ~\cite{Chen2023 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-073: However, the specific choices are rarely justified or even commented on.
elements.sent-2411-074: A good overview of the mix and match regarding surrogate and sampling algorithm can be found in the review of \citet{Hou2021 on Bayesian inference for building energy models.}
elements.sent-2411-075: The review contains an assessment both of surrogate models and sampling algorithms in isolation, but does not cover the integration of both components into a single framework.
elements.sent-2411-076: Especially for active learning strategies, the interplay between the surrogate model and the sampling algorithm is crucial: the sampler determines where the surrogate model is queried (step 2), and the surrogate models in turn affects the acceptance probability (step 4), and the subsequent proposal generation (step 1).
elements.sent-2411-077: In this work, we investigate the interplay of surrogate modelling and sampling algorithms for Bayesian inference.
elements.sent-2411-078: We focus on the inference of material parameters in the non-linear regime of a computational mechanics model.
elements.sent-2411-079: We introduce a scalable case study to create a series of inference problems with comparable complexity but increasing stochastic dimensionality.
elements.sent-2411-080: We employ a GP surrogate model for the likelihood and propose to follow the MCMC chain to collect training data.
elements.sent-2411-081: This form of active learning is based on uncertainty of the model prediction, informing our choice of the GP as surrogate model.
elements.sent-2411-082: As we will demonstrate in~\cref{sec:online_vs_offline , active learning is a crucial component of the framework, ruling out most of the other surrogate models.}
elements.sent-2411-083: Using this set of problems we investigate the following aspects of surrogate accelerated Bayesian inference: i) How does the choice of data collection strategy impact the accuracy of the surrogate model? ii) Do superior MCMC algorithms construct better surrogate models? iii) After training the surrogate model, how does the sampling algorithm for the generation of posterior samples impact accuracy and performance?.
elements.sent-2411-084: This section presents a brief introduction to computational mechanics.
elements.sent-2411-085: The deformation of the body within the domain $\mathscr{D}$ with boundary $\mathscr{B}$ is governed by the equilibrium equation, which can be written as \begin{equation}\nabla \cdot \bs{\sigma} = \bs{0} for \, \bs{x} \in \mathscr{D} \label{eq:equilibrium} \bs{u}(\bs{x},t) = \hat{\bs{u}}(\bs{x},t) for \, \bs{x} \in \mathscr{B} \label{eq:dirichlet_bc},\end{equation} where $\bs{\sigma}$ is the stress tensor and inertia effects and external forces are assumed to be negligible.
elements.sent-2411-086: The boundary conditions in \cref{eq:dirichlet_bc enforce a displacement $\hat{\bs{u }$ at the boundaries of the domain.}}
elements.sent-2411-087: The kinematics of the deformation are described by the displacement field $\bs{u (\bs{x )$.}}
elements.sent-2411-088: Assuming small strains, the strain tensor $\bs{\varepsilon}$ is given by \begin{equation}\bs{\varepsilon} = \frac{1}{2} \left( \nabla \bs{u} + \left(\nabla \bs{u} \right)^\top \right). \label{eq:kinematic_relation}\end{equation} This kinematic relation must be linked to the equilibrium equation in \cref{eq:equilibrium .}
elements.sent-2411-089: The constitutive model \begin{equation}\bs{\sigma} = C(\bs{\varepsilon}, \rf), \label{eq:constitutive_model}\end{equation} relates the stresses to the strains and a local material parameter $\beta$.
elements.sent-2411-090: It should be noted that $\beta$ does not represent an evolving internal state as history dependent materials are not considered in this work.
elements.sent-2411-091: In many situations, the local parameter value depends on a set of global parameters $\bs{\theta \in \mathbb{R ^d$ and the location $\bs{x}$, i.e. $\rf = \rf(\bs{x , \bs{\theta )$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-092: Substituting \cref{eq:constitutive_model into \cref{eq:equilibrium gives \begin{equation}\nabla \cdot \left(C\left(\bs{\varepsilon}, \rf \right) \right) = \bs{0} \label{eq:balance_of_linear_momentum_2},\end{equation} which together with \cref{eq:kinematic_relation can be solved for the displacement field $\bs{u (\bs{x )$ and yields the forward model $\mathcal{M (\bs{\theta )$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-093: The material model may depend on multiple parameters that could also exhibit spatial variability.
elements.sent-2411-094: While the extension to the multivariate case is straightforward, we will focus on the scalar case here for simplicity.
elements.sent-2411-095: The choice of constitutive model—be it linear or non-linear, isotropic or anisotropic, and dependent or independent of time—hinges on the specific material and its regime of deformation.
elements.sent-2411-096: Irrespective of the clarity in the nature of the chosen constitutive model, a persistent challenge lies in the inherent uncertainty of material parameters \cite{Rappel2019,Rappel2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-097: These parameters, crucial for accurately predicting material behaviour, are often not directly measurable but must be inferred from experimental data.
elements.sent-2411-098: The task of inferring these parameters frames the inverse problem associated with the forward model~$\mathcal{M}$.
elements.sent-2411-099: Acknowledging the lack of total control over manufacturing processes or the inherent randomness of the material itself, the assumption of homogeneous material properties is generally not justified.
elements.sent-2411-100: In many applications, material properties can vary significantly across different regions of a part and exhibit randomness under repeated production.
elements.sent-2411-101: We are, therefore, interested not just in inferring global material parameters but also how they vary throughout $\mathscr{D}$.
elements.sent-2411-102: However, there is typically some structure to the randomness that we need to consider when inferring the spatial distribution of material properties.
elements.sent-2411-103: A useful way to capture this spatial variability is through random fields, which provide a framework for describing quantities that vary both in space and with some inherent randomness.
elements.sent-2411-104: More specifically, a random field is a collection of random variables associated with points in a physical space, $x \in \mathscr{D \subset \mathbb{R ^D$.}}
elements.sent-2411-105: For simplicity, we can think of a random field as a function $\hat{\rf (\bs{x )$ that assigns random values to each point $\bs{x}$ in the domain.}}
elements.sent-2411-106: Many physical quantities, such as material properties, can be modeled as Gaussian random fields.
elements.sent-2411-107: This means that any set of values the field takes at different points, $\hat{\rf (\bs{x _1), \dots, \hat{\rf (\bs{x _k)$ for $\bs{x _1, \dots \bs{x _k \in \mathscr{D}$, follows a multivariate normal distribution.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-108: This type of field is fully described by two key statistical properties: i) the mean function $\mu_{\hat{\rf (\bs{x )$, which describes the average value of the field at each point, and ii) the covariance function $C_{\hat{\rf (\bs{x , \bs{x ')$, which tells us how values at different points are correlated based on their distance apart.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-109: In practice, the field is expressed as a truncated series expansion in terms of a set of deterministic basis functions $\phi_i(\bs{x )$ with random coefficients $\theta_i$.}
elements.sent-2411-110: By cutting off the series expansion at a finite number of terms $d$, we obtain a finite-dimensional approximation of the random field: \begin{equation}\label{eq:random_field_expansion} \hat{\rf}(\bs{x}) \approx \rf(\bs{x}, \bs{\theta}) = \mu_{\rf}(\bs{x}) + \sum_{i=1}^{d} \phi_i(\bs{x}) \theta_i.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-111: The expansion effectively amounts to a separation of the physical and the stochastic domain~\cite{Sudret2000 .}
elements.sent-2411-112: Since all randomness is now contained in the coefficients $\bs{\theta \in \mathbb{R ^d$, inferring the random field reduces to inferring $\bs{\theta}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-113: Expansions encountered in related works employ a basis of polynomials~\cite{Deveney2023,Marzouk2009 , wavelets~\cite{Nouy2009 , B-splines~\cite{Vigliotti2018 , or Gaussian-process shape functions~\cite{Li1993 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-114: The optimal expansion in terms of the total mean squared error of the truncation compared to the full sum is the Karhunen-Loève (KL) expansion.
elements.sent-2411-115: It is the eigenfunction expansion of the covariance function of the random field~\cite{Ghanem1991 .}
elements.sent-2411-116: For a Gaussian random field, the KL expansion coefficients $\bs{\theta}$ are i.i.d.
elements.sent-2411-117: For the other expansions, the coefficients are normally distributed but correlated.
elements.sent-2411-118: We opt for a radial basis function (RBF) expansion.
elements.sent-2411-119: The basis is given by \begin{equation}\phi_i(\bs{x}) = \exp\left(-\frac{1}{2l^2} \|\bs{x} - \bs{c}_{i}\|^2\right),\end{equation} where $\bs{c _i$ are the centers of the RBFs and $l$ is the length-scale.}
elements.sent-2411-120: Each basis function $\phi_i$ reaches its maximum at $\bs{c _i$ and decays with increasing distance from the center.}
elements.sent-2411-121: This choice is not optimal in terms of the total mean squared error for a given number of basis functions.
elements.sent-2411-122: However, it has a clear advantage for defining a sequence of comparable inference problems: the RBF expansion leads to a parameterisation of the field where all coefficients have a similar influence on the global system response.
elements.sent-2411-123: On the other hand, the ordering of the eigenfunctions of the KL expansion according to their eigenvalues ensures that the model is most sensitive to changes in the first few coefficients.
elements.sent-2411-124: Furthermore, we circumvent the need to solve the associated eigenvalue problem.
elements.sent-2411-125: Given that the random field is characterised by a squared exponential covariance function, employing a linear model with RBFs and appropriately setting the RBF length-scale produces a similar covariance structure.
elements.sent-2411-126: It converges to the desired covariance in the limit of infinitely many basis functions~\cite[p.. 84]{Rasmussen2006 .}
elements.sent-2411-127: Note that an RBF model with length-scale $l$ produces a covariance function with effective length-scale $\ell = \sqrt{2 l$~\cite{Mackay1998 .}}
elements.sent-2411-128: For simplicity, we choose the prior of the weights to be i.i.d Gaussian, i.e. $\bs{\theta \sim \mathcal{N (\bs{0 , \sigma_\theta^2 \bs{I )$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-129: It is convenient to store the basis functions in a feature vector $\bs{\phi (\bs{x ) = [\phi_1(\bs{x ), \ldots, \phi_{d (\bs{x )]^\mathsf{T}$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-130: The prior mean of the field then reads \begin{equation}E[\rf(\bs{x}, \bs{\theta})] = E\left[\mu_{\rf}(\bs{x}) + \bs{\phi}(\bs{x})^T \bs{\theta}\right] = \mu_{\rf}(\bs{x}) + \bs{\phi}(\bs{x})^T E[\bs{\theta}] = \mu_{\rf}(\bs{x}),\end{equation} showing the desired mean can be achieved by setting $\mu_{\beta = \mu_{\hat{\beta }$.}}
elements.sent-2411-131: The field's covariance function is given by \begin{equation}cov(\rf(\bs{x}, \bs{\theta}), \rf(\bs{x}', \bs{\theta})) = E[(\rf(\bs{x}, \bs{\theta}) - \mu_{\rf}(\bs{x}))(\rf(\bs{x}', \bs{\theta}) - \mu_{\rf}(\bs{x}'))] = E[\bs{\phi}(\bs{x})^T \bs{\theta} \bs{\theta}^T \bs{\phi}(\bs{x}')] = \bs{\phi}(\bs{x})^T \bs{\phi}(\bs{x}') \sigma_\theta^2.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-133: The prior variance of the weights $\sigma_\theta^2$ can be set to match the variance of the random field of interest.
elements.sent-2411-134: It is then given by \begin{equation}\sigma_\theta^2 = \frac{\sigma_{\hat{\rf}}^2}{\sup \left\{\bs{\phi}(\bs{x})^T \bs{\phi}(\bs{x}), \bs{x} \in \mathscr{D} \right\}}. \label{eq:rbf_variance}\end{equation} The random field parameters, i.e. its mean $\mu_{\hat{\rf }$, variance $\sigma_{\hat{\rf ^2$, and effective length-scale $\ell$, can be inferred along the coefficients $\bs{\theta}$ in a hierarchical Bayesian model.}}}
elements.sent-2411-135: We assume to have prior knowledge of the problem that we can use to set these hyperparameters, as hierarchical modelling is not the focus of this study.

elements.sent-2411-144: The likelihood function $p(\mathcal{D |\bs{\theta )$ expresses how well the model prediction matches the observed data for a given realisation of the parameter vector $\bs{\theta}$.}}

elements.sent-2411-146: Following Bayes theorem, the probability of the parameters $\bs{\theta}$ conditioned on the observations $\mathcal{D}$ reads \begin{equation}\underbrace{p(\bs{\theta}|D)}_{posterior} = \frac{\overbrace{p(D | \bs{\theta})}^{likelihood} \overbrace{p( \bs{\theta})}^{prior}} {\underbrace{p(D)}_{model \, evidence}},\end{equation} where the denominator on the right hand side is the so-called evidence.

elements.sent-2411-163: Extending \cref{eq:observation_model to the multi-output case, we have \begin{equation}\tilde{\bs{u}} = \bs{u} + \bs{\epsilon}_u, \quad \tilde{\bs{f}} = \bs{f} + \bs{\epsilon}_f.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-164: Assuming no correlation between the two types of observations, the likelihood takes the form \begin{equation}p \left(  \bs{u} \bs{f}  \bigg\rvert \, \bs{\theta} \right) = N \left(  \bs{u} \bs{f}  \bigg\rvert \, M(\bs{\theta}),  \bs{\Sigma}_{uu} \bs{0} \bs{0} \bs{\Sigma}_{f\!f} \label{eq:likelihood} \right)\end{equation} where the covariance structures $\bs{\Sigma _{uu}$ and $\bs{\Sigma _{f\!f}$ of the displacement and force observations, respectively, are known a priori.}}
elements.sent-2411-165: It should be noted that the i.i.d.\ noise model choice is based on the assumption that we exactly know the physics of the problem, i.e. there is no model misspecification, and that our measurement devices show no spatial or temporal correlation.
elements.sent-2411-166: This simplification is justified for the scope of this study, as it investigates aspects of the Bayesian inference workflow other than the definition of the likelihood function.
elements.sent-2411-167: In practice, however, a more complex observation model that reflects all sources of uncertainty, as outlined in~\cite{Simoen2013 , should be employed.}

elements.sent-2411-169: MCMC methods are a class of algorithms used to sample from a probability distribution from which direct sampling is difficult, hence they are suitable for Bayesian inference in computational mechanics.
elements.sent-2411-170: A Markov chain that has the desired distribution as its equilibrium distribution is constructed and its states are recorded.
elements.sent-2411-171: The more steps the chain takes, the more the distribution of the states of the chain converges to the target distribution.
elements.sent-2411-172: The RWM algorithm is based on a random walk in the state space, determined by a proposal distribution to generate new states.
elements.sent-2411-173: In the subsequent Metropolis correction, the proposed state is either accepted so that the chain moves, or rejected so that the chain stays in place.
elements.sent-2411-174: This correction is essential for the convergence of the chain to the target distribution and is a core building block for many other MCMC algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-175: Suppose we have a function $f(\bs{\theta )$ that is proportional to our target distribution $p(\bs{\theta |\mathcal{D )$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-176: In our case, this function is the joint distribution of parameters and data $p(\mathcal{D , \bs{\theta )$.}}
elements.sent-2411-177: Once the data is observed, the joint distribution essentially becomes a function of the parameters $\bs{\theta}$ only.
elements.sent-2411-178: Given the current state $\bs{\theta ^n$, a move to $\bs{\theta ^*$ is proposed according to the proposal distribution $q(\bs{\theta ^*|\bs{\theta ^{n )$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-179: Next, the Metropolis-Hastings ratio is calculated as \begin{equation}r(\bs{\theta}^n, \bs{\theta}^*) = \frac{f(\bs{\theta}^*)q(\bs{\theta}^*|\bs{\theta}^{n})}{f(\bs{\theta}^{n})q(\bs{\theta}^{n}|\bs{\theta}^*)}. \label{eq:metropolis-hastings_ratio}\end{equation} The move from $\bs{\theta ^{n}$ to $\bs{\theta ^*$ is then accepted with probability \begin{equation}a(\bs{\theta}^{n}, \bs{\theta}^*) = \min \left( 1, r(\bs{\theta}^{n}, \bs{\theta}^*) \right). \label{eq:acceptance_probability}\end{equation} If the proposal distribution $q(\bs{\theta ^*,\bs{\theta ^{n )$ is symmetric---the normal distribution is a common choice---the ratio simplifies to the Metropolis ratio \begin{equation}r(\bs{\theta}^{n},\bs{\theta}^*) = \frac{f(\bs{\theta}^*)}{f(\bs{\theta}^{n})}, \label{eq:metropolis_ratio}\end{equation} also reflected in the algorithms name.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-180: The Gaussian proposal mechanism can then be written as \begin{equation}\bs{\theta}^* = \bs{\theta}^n + s \sqrt{\bs{M}}\bs{z}^n, \label{eq:gaussian_proposal}\end{equation} where $\bs{M}$ is a positive semi-definite preconditioner matrix which defines the covariance of the proposal distribution, $\bs{z ^n$ is a vector of i.i.d.}
elements.sent-2411-181: The corresponding proposal distribution then is \begin{equation}q(\bs{\theta}^*|\bs{\theta}^n) = N (\bs{\theta}^*| \bs{\theta}^n, s^2 \bs{M}).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-182: The performance of the algorithm can be significantly improved by setting $\bs{M}$ to the sample covariance.

elements.sent-2411-184: The proposal covariance can also be adapted continuously during runtime, as long as the level of adaptation is vanishing with the number of iterations~\cite{Roberts2007 .}
elements.sent-2411-185: Because diminishing adaptiveness is difficult to prove in practice, we set the proposal covariance to the sample covariance only once after the first 5000 MCMC-steps.
elements.sent-2411-186: Until then, we use the prior covariance as preconditioner.

elements.sent-2411-191: However,~\citet{Gelman1997 have shown that the proposal variance $s$ must be scaled with the state space dimensionality $d$ according to $\mathcal{O (d^{-1 )$ to achieve the optimal acceptance rate of $0.234$.}}}

elements.sent-2411-197: The Langevin algorithm~\cite{Roberts2002 derives its proposal mechanism from a discretised Langevin diffusion.}
elements.sent-2411-198: It can be seen as a random walk with a drift term that is proportional to the gradient of the log-density.
elements.sent-2411-199: While the continuous Langevin diffusion has the targeted log-density as its stationary distribution, its discretisation introduces a bias.
elements.sent-2411-200: The Metropolis-adjusted Langevin algorithm (MALA) corrects for this bias by adding a Metropolis-Hastings correction to the mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-201: The proposal mechanism of the MALA is given by \begin{equation}\bs{\theta}^* = \bs{\theta}^n + \frac{s^2}{2} \bs{M} \nabla \ln f(\bs{\theta}^n) + s \sqrt{\bs{M}}\bs{z}^n. \label{eq:mala_proposal}\end{equation} which gives rise to the Gaussian proposal distribution \begin{equation}q(\bs{\theta}^*|\bs{\theta}^n) = N \left(\bs{\theta}^*| \bs{\theta}^n + \frac{s^2}{2} \bs{M} \nabla \ln f(\bs{\theta}^n), s^2 \bs{M} \right).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-202: The MALA proposal is similar to the RWM proposal \cref{eq:gaussian_proposal , but has the additional drift term which is proportional to the gradient of the unnormalised log-density $\ln f(\bs{\theta ^n)$.}}

elements.sent-2411-208: The MALA is known to be more efficient than the RWM algorithm for high-dimensional state spaces, as it can exploit the gradient information to make larger steps in the direction of the mode of the target distribution.
elements.sent-2411-209: We employ a GP surrogate model to approximate the likelihood function $\mathcal{L (\bs{\theta )$ in order to reduce the computational cost associated with likelihood evaluations in the many MCMC iterations.}}
elements.sent-2411-210: GP regression is a non-parametric Bayesian regression method that can be used to model complex, non-linear relationships between input and output variables.
elements.sent-2411-211: A parametric model assumes a specific functional form for the relationship between input and output variables and encodes all the information in a set of learnable parameters.
elements.sent-2411-212: In contrast, the non-parametric GP regression model uses the data directly to make predictions.
elements.sent-2411-213: Instead of defining distributions over the parameters of the predefined basis functions, a GP defines a distribution over functions directly.
elements.sent-2411-214: From a mathematical angle, a GP is a collection of random variables, any finite number of which have a joint Gaussian distribution: \begin{equation}\bs{L} \sim N \left( \bs{L} | \bs{0}, \bs{K}(\bs{\Theta},\bs{\Theta}) \right),\end{equation} where a zero mean is assumed before seeing any data.
elements.sent-2411-215: The covariance matrix $\bs{K (\bs{\Theta ,\bs{\Theta )$ is the collection of the kernel function on all input pairs, i.e. $K_{ij = k(\bs{\theta _i,\bs{\theta _j)$ The matrix specifies the similarities in the output $\bs{\mathcal{L }$ based on their input $\bs{\Theta}$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-216: The kernel in this case is chosen as the squared exponential kernel~\cite{Rasmussen2006 , given by \begin{equation}k(\bs{\theta},\bs{\theta}') = \sigma_{f}^2 \exp \left( -\frac{1}{2 \ell^2} \parallel \bs{\theta} - \bs{\theta}' \parallel^2 \right).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-217: The GP variance $\sigma_{\mathrm{f ^2$ controls the magnitude of the fluctuations around the mean function, and its length scale $\ell$ controls the smoothness of the function.}}
elements.sent-2411-218: We assume the relationship of input and output of our training data to be of the form $\eta_i = \mathcal{L (\bs{\theta _i) + \epsilon_i$, where $\mathcal{L (\bs{\theta )$ is the likelihood function and $\epsilon_i$ is i.i.d.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-219: Although the training data originates from noise-free simulations, we admit a noise term $\sigma_{\mathrm{n ^2 \bs{I}$ for numerical stability and model flexibility.}}
elements.sent-2411-220: We denote $\bs{K (\bs{\Theta ,\bs{\Theta ^*)$ the covariance matrix between the training and test points, and $\bs{K (\bs{\Theta ^*,\bs{\Theta ^*)$ the covariance matrix between the test points.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-221: We can then write the joint distribution over the training output $\bs{\eta}$ and new predictions $\bs{\mathcal{L ^*$ at locations $\bs{\Theta ^*$ as \begin{equation} \bs{\eta} \bs{L}^*  \sim N \left( \bs{0},  \bs{K}(\bs{\Theta},\bs{\Theta}) + \sigma_{n}^2 \bs{I} \bs{K}(\bs{\Theta},\bs{\Theta}^*) \bs{K}(\bs{\Theta}^*,\bs{\Theta}) \bs{K}(\bs{\Theta}^*,\bs{\Theta}^*)  \right).\end{equation}.}}}
elements.sent-2411-222: Applying Bayes' formula, the predictive distribution of the test output $\bs{\mathcal{L ^* = \bs{\mathcal{L (\bs{\Theta ^*)$ given the training data $\mathcal{D _{\mathrm{GP = (\bs{\Theta , \bs{\eta )$ is then given by \begin{equation}\bs{L}^* | \bs{\eta}, \bs{\Theta}, \bs{\Theta}^* \sim N \left( \bs{L}^* | \bs{\mu}^*, \bs{\Sigma}^* \right), \bs{\mu}^* = \bs{K}(\bs{\Theta}^*,\bs{\Theta}) \left( \bs{K}(\bs{\Theta},\bs{\Theta}) + \sigma_{n}^2 \bs{I} \right)^{-1} \bs{\eta},\nonumber \bs{\Sigma}^* = \bs{K}(\bs{\Theta}^*,\bs{\Theta}^*) - \bs{K}(\bs{\Theta}^*,\bs{\Theta}) \left( \bs{K}(\bs{\Theta},\bs{\Theta}) + \sigma_{n}^2 \bs{I} \right)^{-1} \bs{K}(\bs{\Theta},\bs{\Theta}^*). \nonumber\end{equation} The predictive mean $\bs{\mu ^*$ is our best guess about the function values at the test points, and the predictive variance $\bs{\Sigma ^*$ quantifies our uncertainty about these predictions.}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-223: We determine the GP model hyperparameters---the process variance $\sigma_{\mathrm{f ^2$, the noise variance $\sigma_{\mathrm{n ^2$, and the length scale $\ell$---via empirical Bayes for computational efficiency.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-224: For this, we maximise the marginal likelihood of the training outputs $\bs{\eta}$ given the training input $\bs{\Theta}$.
elements.sent-2411-225: The marginal likelihood $p(\bs{\eta | \sigma_{\mathrm{f ^2, \sigma_{\mathrm{n ^2, \ell)$ and its logarithm $L$ are given by \begin{equation}p(\bs{\eta} | \sigma_{f}^2, \sigma_{n}^2, \ell) = \int p(\bs{\eta} | \bs{L}, \sigma_{n}^2) p(\bs{L} | \sigma_{f}^2, \ell) d\bs{L}, \label{eq:marginal_likelihood} L = \ln p(\bs{\eta} | \sigma_{f}^2, \sigma_{n}^2, \ell).\label{eq:log_marginal_likelihood}\end{equation} Instead of maximising the marginal likelihood directly, we maximise the log marginal likelihood \cref{eq:log_marginal_likelihood to avoid numerical issues.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-226: The associated optimisation problem is solved with the BFGS algorithm~\cite{Fletcher2000 .}
elements.sent-2411-227: Due to the non-convex nature of the optimisation problem, the optimisation is performed multiple times with different initial guesses for the hyperparameters to ensure convergence to the global or a sufficiently good local maximum.
elements.sent-2411-228: The obtained point estimates for the hyperparameters are then used for the subsequent prediction.
elements.sent-2411-229: In this section, we introduce an algorithm that employs MCMC-guided sampling as a straightforward active learning strategy for constructing surrogate models.
elements.sent-2411-230: This approach leverages the predictive uncertainty inherent in the GP surrogate model, coupled with the trajectory of the MCMC algorithm, drawing inspiration from the active learning framework presented in~\citet{Rocha2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-231: Initially, we generate a set of $N_0$ samples from the posterior distribution using the forward model.
elements.sent-2411-232: This set forms the initial training dataset for our GP surrogate model.
elements.sent-2411-233: Upon this dataset, we fit the GP model by estimating its hyperparameters and record the initial log marginal likelihood, denoted as $L_{\mathrm{old }$.}
elements.sent-2411-234: After the initialisation phase is completed, the MCMC algorithm generates new proposals based on the GP model.
elements.sent-2411-235: Denoting $\bs{k (\bs{\theta ^*)$ the covariance vector between the training data and the new point $\bs{\theta ^*$, and $\bs{K}$ the covariance matrix of the training data, the predictive variance of the GP model at $\bs{\theta ^*$ is given by \begin{equation}V[L(\bs{\theta}^*)] = k(\bs{\theta}^*, \bs{\theta}^*) - \bs{k}(\bs{\theta}^*)^\top\left(\bs{K} + \sigma^2\bs{I}\right)^{-1}\bs{k}(\bs{\theta}^*) \label{eq:gp_predictive_point_variance_1}.\end{equation}.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-236: If this variance remains below a specified threshold $\gamma_{\mathrm{v }$, we accept the GP's prediction at $\bs{\theta ^*$ and proceed without further action.}}
elements.sent-2411-237: Conversely, if the predictive variance exceeds the variance threshold $\gamma_{\mathrm{v }$, we resort to evaluating the forward model $\bs{y ^* = \mathcal{M (\bs{\theta ^*)$ and compute the likelihood $\mathcal{L (\bs{\theta ^*)$ to obtain a new observation.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-238: We will refer to $\gamma_{\mathrm{v }$ as the reject threshold.}
elements.sent-2411-239: This observation is then used to update our training dataset.
elements.sent-2411-240: Following the addition of new data, we update the GP's posterior covariance matrix and recalculate the log marginal likelihood, denoted as $L_{\mathrm{new }$.}
elements.sent-2411-241: If the ratio $\left| \nicefrac{L_{\mathrm{new {L_{\mathrm{old \right|$ exceeds a predetermined threshold $\gamma_{\mathrm{L }$, this indicates a significant change in the model's understanding, prompting a re-estimation of the GP hyperparameters based on the updated dataset.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-242: We will thus refer to $\gamma_{\mathrm{L }$ as retrain threshold.}
elements.sent-2411-243: The process iterates until the end of the burn-in phase $N_{\mathrm{b }$ is reached, at which point the GP model's hyperparameters are fixed.}
elements.sent-2411-244: These parameters have a significant impact on the model's predictive accuracy and their choice is therefore investigated in \cref{sec:active-learning-validation .}
elements.sent-2411-245: Replacing the forward model with the GP surrogate model leads to a modified proposal distribution \cref{eq:mala_proposal for the MALA: \begin{equation}\tilde{p}(\bs{\theta}^* | \bs{\theta}) = N \left( \bs{\theta}^* | \bs{\theta}^n + \frac{s^2}{2} \bs{M}^{-1} \nabla_{\bs{\theta}} \left( E \left[ L(\bs{\theta}^n) \right]+ \ln p(\bs{\theta}^n) \right), s^2 \bs{M}\right). \label{eq:modified-mala-proposal}\end{equation} The modified MALA acceptance probability is then given by: \begin{equation}\tilde{a}(\bs{\theta}^n, \bs{\theta}^*) = \min \left( 1, \frac{\exp \left (E\left[L(\bs{\theta}^*)\right] \right) p(\bs{\theta}^*) \tilde{q}(\bs{\theta}^*|\bs{\theta}^{n})} {\exp \left( E \left[L(\bs{\theta}^{n})\right] \right) p(\bs{\theta}^n) \tilde{q}(\bs{\theta}^{n}|\bs{\theta}^*)} \right) \label{eq:modified-mala-acceptance}\end{equation} The proposal for the RWM algorithm \cref{eq:gaussian_proposal remains the same, as it does not depend on the forward model.}}
elements.sent-2411-246: However, the acceptance probability \cref{eq:acceptance_probability is modified to account for the GP surrogate model: \begin{equation}\tilde{a}(\bs{\theta}^n, \bs{\theta}^*) = \min \left( 1, \frac{\exp \left (E\left[L(\bs{\theta}^*)\right] \right) p(\bs{\theta}^*)} {\exp \left( E \left[L(\bs{\theta}^{n})\right] \right) p(\bs{\theta}^n)} \right) \label{eq:modified-rwm-acceptance}\end{equation} The MCMC algorithm incurs a bias from the surrogate model in the acceptance probabilities \cref{eq:modified-mala-acceptance,eq:modified-rwm-acceptance .}}
elements.sent-2411-247: The quantification of this bias and its impact on the posterior estimation is at the heart of this work.
elements.sent-2411-248: As the GP model refines its accuracy with the incorporation of new data, we anticipate a reduction in predictive variance, signaling an enhanced model fidelity.
elements.sent-2411-249: Consequently, the need to resort to the computationally expensive forward model diminishes.
elements.sent-2411-250: This strategy ensures that new data points are strategically added to the model's training set, particularly focusing on regions yet to be explored by the MCMC algorithm, without changing the response surface in regions that have been visited in previous steps.
elements.sent-2411-251: The active learning strategy is described in detail in~\cref{alg:active_learning .}
elements.algorithm-2411-001: \begin{algorithmic}[1] \State Initialise $N_0$ samples from the posterior using the forward model to obtain initial dataset $\mathcal{D}_{\mathrm{GP}} = \{(\bs{\theta}_0, \mathcal{L}(\bs{\theta}_0)), \dots, (\bs{\theta}_N, \mathcal{L}(\bs{\theta}_{N_0}))\}$ \State Estimate hyperparameters on $\mathcal{D}_{\mathrm{GP}}$ and record $L_{\mathrm{old}}$ \State $n \gets 0$ \While{$n < N_{\mathrm{total}}$} \State Generate new point $\bs{\theta}^*$ from the proposal distribution $\tilde{q}(\bs{\theta}^*|\bs{\theta}^n)$ \label{alg:proposal} \If{$\mathbb{V}[\mathcal{L}(\bs{\theta}^*)] < \gamma_{\mathrm{v}}$} \State Accept GP prediction for $\mathcal{L}(\bs{\theta}^*)$ \Else \State Evaluate forward model $\bs{y}^* = \mathcal{M}(\bs{\theta}^*)$, compute likelihood $\mathcal{L}(\bs{\theta}^*)$ \State Update training dataset $\mathcal{D}_{\mathrm{GP}} \gets \mathcal{D}_{\mathrm{GP}} \cup \left( \bs{\theta}^*, \mathcal{L}(\bs{\theta}^*) \right)$ \State Update GP's posterior covariance matrix and compute $L_{\mathrm{new}}$ \If{$\left| \nicefrac{L_{\mathrm{new}}}{L_{\mathrm{old}}} \right| > \gamma_{\mathrm{L}}$ AND $n < N_{\mathrm{b}}$} \State Re-estimate GP hyperparameters and update $L_{\mathrm{new}}$ \State $L_{\mathrm{old}} \gets L_{\mathrm{new}}$ \EndIf \EndIf \If{$\tilde{a}(\bs{\theta}^n, \bs{\theta}^*) > u \sim \mathcal{U}(0,1)$} \label{alg:acceptance} \State $\bs{\theta}^{n+1} \gets \bs{\theta}^*$ \Else \State $\bs{\theta}^{n+1} \gets \bs{\theta}^n$ \EndIf \State $n \gets n + 1$ \EndWhile \end{algorithmic} \caption{MCMC-guided active learning for GP surrogate model construction. Depending on the choice of the algorithm, the proposal density $\tilde{q}(\bs{\theta}^*|\bs{\theta}^n)$ in \cref{alg:proposal} is either given by \cref{eq:gaussian_proposal} for the RWM algorithm or \cref{eq:modified-mala-proposal} for the MALA. Similarily, the acceptance probabiliy $\tilde{\alpha}$ in \cref{alg:acceptance} is given by \cref{eq:modified-rwm-acceptance} (RWM) or \cref{eq:modified-mala-acceptance} (MALA).} \label{alg:active_learning}

elements.sent-2411-257: To make things more challenging, we introduce a simple non-linear constitutive model and a spatially varying initial stiffness.
elements.sent-2411-258: It is adopted as a fast and pragmatic choice, but nevertheless without loss of generality.
elements.sent-2411-259: The constitutive model is given by \begin{equation}\sigma (\varepsilon) = r (E - H) \left( 1 - exp \left(-\frac{\varepsilon}{r} \right) \right) + H \varepsilon. \label{eq:expl_material_model}\end{equation} This model, characterised by its initial stiffness $E$, its terminal stiffness $H$, and its rate parameter $r$, mimics nonlinear material behaviour during plastic deformation.
elements.sent-2411-260: It does so without necessitating the computational burden associated with tracking the evolution of plastic strain.
elements.sent-2411-261: Note that this simplification is only reasonable for the monotonic loading considered in this study.
elements.sent-2411-262: We thereby embed additional non-linearity into the system without the exhaustive computational requirements typical of a complete plasticity model.

The three subplots show the stress-strain relationship for different values of the material parameters.} \label{fig:material_model}
elements.sent-2411-263: The material model's non-linearity is evident, with the stress-strain curve exhibiting a distinct hardening behaviour.
elements.sent-2411-264: After initial numerical testing, no single parameter stood out as comparatively more challenging to infer, except for a hardening modulus $H$ close to zero.
elements.sent-2411-265: We, therefore, focus exclusively on the initial stiffness $E$ in the subsequent analysis to ensure a consistent comparison across the different methods at fixed $H$ and $r$.
elements.sent-2411-266: The spatially varying initial stiffness $\hat{\beta = \hat{E = \hat{E (x) \approx E(x, \bs{\theta )$ is hence the unknown field to be inferred.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-267: We define a sequence of inverse problems by considering different stochastic discretisations of the spatially varying initial stiffness.
elements.sent-2411-268: Taking the spatial correlation of the RBF discretisation into account, further additions of basis functions are expected to yield diminishing gains of information for a given random field.
elements.sent-2411-269: As the RBFs with centers close to one another are expected to correlate, their inference will be less challenging.
elements.sent-2411-270: We adapt the length scale of the random field, and thereby the complexity of the inference problem, according to the number of terms in the random field expansion, as demonstrated in \cref{fig:problem_sequence .}
elements.sent-2411-271: In other words, we must infer a random function with an expected number of zero crossings proportional to the number of free parameters.
elements.sent-2411-272: We also increase the amount of displacement data to ensure that the observations-to-parameters ratio remains constant.
elements.sent-2411-273: This ensures any difference in inference performance as the dimensionality of the latent space increases can be ascribed to the dimensionality and not to the complexity of the inferred behaviour.

elements.sent-2411-292: The goal is now to infer the parameters of a matching discretisation of the random field from these noisy observations.
elements.sent-2411-293: So far, we have introduced a scalable case study, two MCMC algorithms (RWM and MALA) to solve the inverse problems, and a GP surrogate model that can facilitate this process.
elements.sent-2411-294: To understand the impact of the methodological decisions on the inference process, we must compare the resulting approximate posterior distributions.
elements.sent-2411-295: However, neither our ground truth nor the surrogate-model-assisted inference are available in analytical form, but instead are represented with samples from MCMC simulations.
elements.sent-2411-296: We must therefore find a suitable metric to compare these empirical distributions.
elements.sent-2411-297: The Wasserstein distance between two distributions is the minimum effort required to morph one into the other.
elements.sent-2411-298: This metric is particularly advantageous for empirical distributions, such as those represented by MCMC samples, because it can be directly applied to point clouds without requiring the distributions to be expressed in a functional form.
elements.sent-2411-299: Other metrics, such as the Kullback-Leibler divergence or the total variation distance inherently require at least one of the distributions to be functionally described.
elements.sent-2411-300: To use these metrics in the given settings, one must approximate the empirical distribution using kernel density estimation or another density estimation technique, which introduces additional complexity and uncertainty into the analysis.
elements.sent-2411-301: In contrast, the Wasserstein distance directly considers the actual distances between points avoiding the pitfalls associated with density estimation.
elements.sent-2411-302: For our case study, we specifically employ the Wasserstein 2-distance, which is the square root of the optimal transport cost, and denote it as $W_2$ in the following.
elements.sent-2411-303: The Wasserstein 2-distance $W_2(P, Q)$ between two continuous distributions $P$ and $Q$ on a metric space $\mathcal{X}$ is defined as: \begin{equation}W_2(P, Q) = \left( \inf_{\gamma \in \Gamma(P, Q)} \int_{X \times X} \|x - y\|^2 \, d\gamma(x, y) \right)^{\frac{1}{2}},\end{equation} where $\Gamma(P, Q)$ is the set of all joint distributions on $\mathcal{X \times \mathcal{X}$ with marginals $P$ and $Q$.}
elements.formula-2411-041: \begin{equation}W_2(P, Q) = \left( \inf_{\gamma \in \Gamma(P, Q)} \int_{X \times X} \|x - y\|^2 \, d\gamma(x, y) \right)^{\frac{1}{2}},\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-304: In case of empirical distributions represented by an equal amount of samples $N_{\mathrm{total }$, the Wasserstein 2-distance reduces to: \begin{equation}W_2(P, Q) = \left( \frac{1}{N_{total}} \sum_{i=1}^{N_{total}} \|x_i - y_{\pi(i)}\|^2 \right)^{\frac{1}{2}},\end{equation} where $x_i$ and $y_i$ are the samples from the distributions $P$ and $Q$, respectively, and $\pi$ is the permutation that minimises the square distance.}
elements.sent-2411-305: In this section, we present the results for the case study.
elements.sent-2411-306: We first demonstrate the inference on the bar problem in general.
elements.sent-2411-307: Next, we validate the active learning approach on the five-dimensional problem and compare it to the a priori training strategies.
elements.sent-2411-308: Finally, we look at the impact of the MCMC algorithm on the surrogate model construction and on the efficiency and accuracy of the inference.
elements.sent-2411-309: All results are obtained using the RWM algorithm exclusively relying on the FEM model throughout the MCMC run.
elements.sent-2411-310: In these and all other runs, a total of \num{200000 MCMC samples were generated for each run, with the first \num{5000 samples discarded as burn-in.}}
elements.sent-2411-311: Further, each chain was thinned by a factor of 40 to ensure uncorrelated posterior samples.
elements.sent-2411-312: This yields a total of \num{4500 samples per posterior distribution, keeping the cost of computing the Wasserstein distance manageable.}
elements.sent-2411-313: The posterior distributions of the initial stiffness are in good agreement with the ground truth in all scenarios presented in \cref{fig:posterior_fields , underscoring the efficacy of the Bayesian approach.}
elements.sent-2411-314: Further, the true fields are well contained within the 95\% credible interval of the posterior.
elements.sent-2411-315: This consistency indicates a robust and meaningful quantification of the uncertainty, affirming the reliability of our inference process.
elements.sent-2411-316: With this non-linear, yet scalable sequence of inverse problems at hand, we now turn our attention to the many modelling choices in surrogate model assisted inference.

elements.sent-2411-329: This section explores the training strategies for surrogate models, specifically contrasting the online active learning approach with conventional offline a priori methods.
elements.sent-2411-330: In a priori learning, the training dataset is predetermined before the inference process begins, without considering the data observed during this process.
elements.sent-2411-331: We examine three predominant strategies for compiling this dataset:.
elements.sent-2411-332: Grid: the training data forms a structured grid;.
elements.sent-2411-333: LHS: the training data is uniformly distributed and sampled according to a Latin hypercube design~\cite{McKay1979 ;.}
elements.sent-2411-334: Prior: the training data is sampled from the prior distribution of the material parameters.
elements.sent-2411-335: Despite the simplicity of sampling from the Gaussian prior, both Grid and LHS strategies encounter an implementation challenge due to the prior's infinite support.
elements.sent-2411-336: Attempting to impose a uniform distribution across this boundless domain inherently results in an improper distribution with zero density, rendering traditional sampling methods impractical.
elements.sent-2411-337: To address this, we implement a cutoff mechanism to define a finite bounding box in the latent space, enabling meaningful sampling for Grid and LHS methods.
elements.sent-2411-338: We set this cutoff at three standard deviations from the prior mean in each dimension, prioritising comprehensive coverage over the prior's support.
elements.sent-2411-339: Although this approach may slightly compromise predictive accuracy within the denser regions of the prior, it significantly enhances model performance in instances where a notable discrepancy exists between the prior and posterior distributions.
elements.sent-2411-340: The active learning approach of \cref{sec:mcmc_guided_active_learning , on the other hand, dynamically adjusts the training dataset based on the observed data, thereby adapting to the posterior distribution.}
elements.sent-2411-341: Therefore, it self-determines the amount and location of the training data points.
elements.sent-2411-342: All four sampling strategies are depicted in \cref{fig:train_data_2d .}
elements.sent-2411-343: We now want to compare the accuracy of the surrogate models trained with the four different training data collection strategies.
elements.sent-2411-344: To this end, we consider the range of latent space dimensionalites $d \in \{2, 3, 4, 5\}$ and the associated inverse problems.
elements.sent-2411-345: For the a priori learning approaches, we set the number of training data points to $N_\mathrm{train = 5^d$ for an even coverage in all directions.}
elements.sent-2411-346: The GP-hyperparameters are estimated and fixed for the subsequent inference process.
elements.sent-2411-347: Each setting is repeated 10 times with different ground truths to account for the stochastic nature of the training process and the MCMC\@.
elements.sent-2411-348: The GP hyperparameter optimisation is performed 20 times with different initialisations.
elements.figure-2411-008: \includegraphics[width=0.95\textwidth]{train_modes_2d_visualisation} \caption{Visualisation of the training data for the 2-dimensional problem. The left subplot shows the training data for the active learning approach, while the remaining subplots show the training data for the a priori learning approaches.} \label{fig:train_data_2d}
elements.sent-2411-349: We observe that for a latent space with dimensionality of $d=2$ and $d=3$, the performance across all strategies is relatively similar, albeit with grid sampling showing a slight disadvantage.
elements.sent-2411-350: This parity shifts as we move to higher dimensions ($d=4$ and $d=5$), where the active learning strategy outperforms the a priori methods.
elements.sent-2411-351: Moreover, a priori learning approaches demonstrate a broader variance in approximation error; some iterations are nearly as effective as active learning, while others fall markedly short.
elements.sent-2411-352: This variability is largely attributable to the spatial distribution of training data points with respect to regions of high posterior probability.
elements.sent-2411-353: In lower-dimensional spaces, the probability of a priori training data aligning with these critical regions remains high.
elements.sent-2411-354: However, as the dimensionality increases, the likelihood of such fortuitous alignment decreases significantly.
elements.sent-2411-355: This diminishing probability underlies the observed trends in approximation error: outcomes are either closely aligned with those of the active learning approach or significantly divergent, with few instances falling in between.
elements.sent-2411-356: These results underscore the increasing advantage of active learning in higher-dimensional spaces, attributed to its strategic adaptability in navigating the complexities introduced by the curse of dimensionality.

elements.sent-2411-360: Nonetheless, the active learning now clearly outperforms the a priori approaches for all latent space dimensionalities.
elements.sent-2411-362: As to why the a priori learning approaches perform worse than the active learning approach, it is helpful to inspect the different posteriors resulting from a single run with $d=3$ in \cref{fig:inspection_seed-9 .}

elements.sent-2411-373: After exposing the deficiencies of the a priori training data collection strategies, we settle on the active learning approach for the remainder of the experiments.
elements.sent-2411-374: In this section, we consider the MALA alongside the RWM algorithm, and compare their impact on surrogate model construction, sampling efficiency, and accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-375: We investigate the performance of the MALA and the RWM algorithm across a range of latent space dimensionalities, from $d=2$ to $d=15$.
elements.sent-2411-376: Every setting was repeated with 50 different ground-truth realisations (\cref{fig:problem_sequence ) to account for the inherent randomness of the MCMC algorithms.}
elements.sent-2411-377: The GP hyperparameter optimisation is performed 50 times with different initialisations.
elements.sent-2411-378: We begin by evaluating the number of training data points across various latent space dimensions and for three distinct reject thresholds $\gamma_{\mathrm{v }$.}
elements.sent-2411-379: Since the proposal mechanism of the sampling algorithms drives the training data acquisition, the MALA and the RWM algorithm construct different surrogate models.
elements.sent-2411-380: The MALA requires fewer training data points than the RWM algorithm across all latent space dimensionalities (\cref{fig:surrogate_model_construction ) under stringent uncertainty requirements.}
elements.sent-2411-381: With the added value of the gradient information, the MALA generates more informative proposals, which are more likely to be accepted.
elements.sent-2411-382: Therefore, the MALA requires fewer steps to generate sufficient training data for the surrogate model.
elements.sent-2411-383: The MALA advantage regarding the training data is persistent across all $d$, shown by the constant offset to RWM in log-space.
elements.sent-2411-384: The slopes of both algorithms, however, are similar, indicating that they are subject to the curse of dimensionality in a similar manner.
elements.sent-2411-385: The right column of \cref{fig:surrogate_model_construction details the accuracy of the inferences.}
elements.sent-2411-386: Under strict uncertainty controls ($\gamma_{\mathrm{v =1.0$), both RWM and MALA achieve similar levels of accuracy in approximating the posterior distribution.}}
elements.sent-2411-387: Outliers below the trend line in \cref{fig:n_data_rej-1.0 correspond to the outliers with high $W_2$ in \cref{fig:wd_rej-1.0 , highlighting the tradeoff between accuracy and efficiency.}}
elements.sent-2411-388: These outliers suggest a superior robustness of active learning with the RWM, as they exclusively occurred with the MALA.
elements.sent-2411-389: With looser uncertainty requirements these outliers become more prevalent (\cref{fig:n_data_rej-5.0,fig:wd_rej-5.0 ) and eventually they become the norm (\cref{fig:n_data_rej-20.0,fig:wd_rej-20.0 ).}}
elements.sent-2411-390: What happens in these MALA runs is that the initial MCMC samples are too far removed from the posterior's high-density regions.
elements.sent-2411-391: These samples then poorly represent the likelihood's complexity, potentially leading to an overestimated GP length scale and an overly confident surrogate model that ceases to collect more training data.
elements.sent-2411-392: While the RMW algorithm encounters the same issue, it is less pronounced due to the more exploratory nature of the random walk proposal: eventually the algorithm moves towards the high-density regions of the posterior by chance, leading to further collection of training data.
elements.sent-2411-393: The MALA, however, is misled by an erroneous surrogate model and its poor approximation of the gradient and cannot correct itself.
elements.sent-2411-394: To summarise the findings, the RWM algorithm and the MALA perform similarly both in terms of the number of training data points and the accuracy of the inference when strong requirements are imposed on the uncertainty in the surrogate model.
elements.sent-2411-395: When the uncertainty tolerance is relaxed, the RWM still produces accurate surrogates, underlining its superior robustness, while the accuracy of the surrogates trained with the MALA deteriorates.

elements.sent-2411-396: While the MCMC algorithms are guaranteed to converge to the distribution given by the surrogate model, the surrogate model itself must also converge to the true likelihood function.
elements.sent-2411-397: To investigate the level of adaptation of the surrogate model, we analyze the number of new data points collected throughout the MCMC chain.
elements.sent-2411-398: The cumulative number of data points collected for both algorithms is shown in \cref{subfig:adaptation_rwm_cum and \cref{subfig:adaptation_mala_cum .}}
elements.sent-2411-399: Three conclusions can be drawn: i) the rate of training data collection decreases in all cases, ii) the rate is higher for the RMW algorithm than for the MALA, iii) the rate depends on latent space dimensionality---the higher the dimensionality, the more data points are needed at all stages of the chain.
elements.sent-2411-400: Even though the rate reduces to one new data point per 2000 MCMC steps towards the end of the $d = 10$ case, this dependence suggests a stationary surrogate model becomes more difficult to obtain with increasing latent space dimensionality.
elements.figure-2411-013: \subcaptionbox{RWM rate \label{subfig:adaptation_rwm_rate}}[0.48\textwidth]{ \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{mh_collect_times} } \subcaptionbox{MALA rate \label{subfig:adaptation_mala_rate}}[0.48\textwidth]{ \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{mala_collect_times} } \subcaptionbox{RWM cumulative \label{subfig:adaptation_rwm_cum}}[0.48\textwidth]{ \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{mh_collect_times_total_plot_log} } \subcaptionbox{MALA cumulative \label{subfig:adaptation_mala_cum}}[0.48\textwidth]{ \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{mala_collect_times_total_plot_log} } \caption{Level of adaptation of the MCMC algorithms RWM in \cref{subfig:adaptation_rwm_rate} and MALA in \cref{subfig:adaptation_mala_rate}. Each bar represents the number of new data points collected within intervals of 2000 MCMC steps, averaged over 50 realisations. The latent space dimensionality is color coded.} \label{fig:adaptation}
elements.subsubsection-2411-006: Sampling Performance Given a Trained Surrogate Model
elements.sent-2411-401: Finally, we compare the sampling accuracy and efficiency of the RWM algorithm and the MALA when using the same surrogate model.
elements.sent-2411-402: In this case, we are using the one constructed by the RWM algorithm with $\gamma_{\mathrm{v = 1.0$ and prohibit further training data collection.}}
elements.sent-2411-403: The numerical experiment is repeated for the same 50 distinct realisations of the inverse problem per latent space dimensionality as in \cref{sec:surrogate-model-construction .}
elements.sent-2411-404: There is no clear difference in accuracy of the posterior approximation between the MALA and the RWM algorithm for any given dimensionality.
elements.sent-2411-405: Starting from $d = 10$, we observe an increasing number of outliers with high $W_2$ values.
elements.sent-2411-406: These outliers represent chains that have completely diverged from regions with high posterior density.
elements.sent-2411-407: These chains navigated to largely unexplored regions of the parameter space, as indicated by the large predictive variance encountered during the respective MCMC run.
elements.sent-2411-408: Runs with at least one occurrence of a maximum predictive variance five times larger than $\gamma_{\mathrm{v }$ are marked with a cross, which match well with the outliers in \cref{fig:sampler_influence .}}
elements.sent-2411-409: This phenomenon occurs for both algorithms, but is more pronounced for the RWM algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-410: It underscores the shortcomings of a priori trained surrogate models in high-dimensional settings, as even a model tailored to a given posterior cannot guarantee the accuracy of the inference.

The crosses in indicate the outliers with predicive variance.} \label{fig:sampler_influence}
elements.sent-2411-411: In this research, we have examined the integration of surrogate modelling with Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampling techniques to improve the efficiency of Bayesian model calibration.
elements.sent-2411-412: We introduced a simple, scalable one-dimensional bar problem equipped with a non-linear constitutive model and spatially varying stiffness.
elements.sent-2411-413: The stiffness field is discretised with radial basis functions, the number of which can be adapted to control the dimensionality of the latent space.
elements.sent-2411-414: This problem serves as our test bed to explore the impacts of various methodological decisions associated with MCMC and surrogate applications.
elements.sent-2411-415: We employed a Gaussian Process (GP) surrogate model to approximate the log likelihood function and evaluated several traditional a priori training strategies: Latin hypercube sampling, uniform grid sampling, and sampling from the prior distribution.
elements.sent-2411-416: Furthermore, we proposed an active learning strategy that adaptively selects training data points based on the MCMC path, leveraging the GP model's inherent uncertainty quantification capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-417: Our findings underscore the limitations of traditional a priori methods and demonstrate the superiority of active learning in balancing computational costs with analytical accuracy, particularly for problems with higher stochastic dimensionality.
elements.sent-2411-418: Comparative analysis of the random walk Metropolis (RWM) and Metropolis-adjusted Langevin algorithm (MALA) in terms of surrogate model construction, sampling efficiency, and inference accuracy revealed that both algorithms perform comparably under stringent surrogate model uncertainty requirements.
elements.sent-2411-419: The increased sampling efficiency of the MALA was beneficial for the training of the surrogate model.
elements.sent-2411-420: The higher quality of the MALA samples lead to the construction of a surrogate model with similar accuracy to the RWM algorithm, but with fewer FEM model evaluations.
elements.sent-2411-421: However, under relaxed conditions, accuracy was better maintained with the RWM algorithm than with the MALA, underscoring the robustness of the RWM algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-422: When a predefined surrogate model was used, the choice of the sampling algorithm did not affect inference accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-423: However, there is a notable chance for both algorithms to depart from the regions of high surrogate-model certainty and accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-424: These divergent chains could not return to the high-density regions, even for surrogate models tailored to the posterior distribution, which further emphasised the shortcomings of a priori training.
elements.sent-2411-425: This study suggest that related works~\cite{Deveney2023, YuanHu2024, Thomas2022, Chen2023 incorrectly identify the MCMC algorithm as a bottleneck in the inference process.}
elements.sent-2411-426: The intensive training data requirements for constructing a surrogate model in high-dimensional scenarios render the MCMC-with-surrogate approach impractical before any significant benefits of advanced sampling algorithms can be realised.
elements.sent-2411-427: Assuming a forward evaluation of our simulator takes a mere 10 min, the compilation of the training dataset for a 15-dimensional latent space would require roughly 70 days.
elements.sent-2411-428: Any gains in sampling efficiency from advanced MCMC algorithms would be overshadowed by the prohibitive costs of surrogate model construction.
elements.sent-2411-429: This finding is at odds with common practice, where engineers often put significant resources into the integration and analysis of advanced MCMC algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-430: This study clearly suggests prioritising the surrogate model construction over the choice of sampling algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-431: Further research into multi-fidelity MCMC schemes~\cite{Zhang2018 and nonlinear dimensionality reduction techniques~\cite{Dasgupta2024 could provide viable strategies to mitigate the curse of dimensionality.}}
